pageTitle.value: Lin-Manuel Miranda & Agustina San Martín - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Lin-Manuel Miranda and his protégée Agustina San Martín met while he was making his film directorial debut.

WaPageTitle.value: Lin-Manuel Miranda & Agustina San Martín - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Rolex mentor Lin-Manuel Miranda has helped his protégée Agustina San Martín grow as an artist by supporting her creative process. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Lin-Manuel Miranda and his protégée Agustina San Martín met while he was making his film directorial debut. There was a bolt of artistic recognition between the two artists that allowed a true collaboration.

“I think she is after the truth,” says Lin-Manuel Miranda of his protégée, Argentinian filmmaker Agustina San Martín. “And you just can’t teach that. I have met a lot of artists who are clever, but who don’t have anything to say. Agustina’s art is infused with her curiosity and the essence of what she is after. I am really excited to see what she makes.”

He smiles broadly. His relationship with San Martín has developed into one of mutual support. “I see her as a very cherished colleague,” he says. San Martín adds, appreciatively: “When we start speaking, we just have fun with the thing that we are talking about. I think that ping-pong of words gives us a very interesting way of connecting.”

On some levels, Miranda and San Martín were not the most obvious pairing as mentor and protégé. He is the creator of the Tony Award-winning musicals Hamilton and In the Heights and songwriter for the hugely popular animated feature films Vivo, Moana and Encanto. Her early work, made in her native Buenos Aires, which included the Cannes award-winning Monster God and her first feature To Kill the Beast, was dark, scary, mysterious and esoteric.

The difference has acted as a creative stimulus for them both. “I think the contrast between us was the most nurturing thing,” says San Martín. Yet, as Miranda points out, from the moment he chose her to be his protégée as part of the Rolex mentoring programme, he also knew they shared a tenacious energy and a vision. He says: “There was just that connection instantly. I felt like I was having a creative meeting on some project.”

From the outset, Miranda had been determined to widen the doors and create opportunities for artists of colour through his mentorship. “The more diverse storytellers we have, the more diverse stories we get, and we just get better movies as a result. I am excited to be part of that and pay it forward in that way.”

He had also made clear he wanted to work with a filmmaker because he would be directing his first film, tick tick… BOOM!, during the mentorship. “I said to her: ‘You’ve made more movies than me!’ She ended up being an invaluable sounding board during the process of directing my first movie. Agustina is such a vivid, poetic and imaginative thinker, and that was wonderful for me. Because, when you are in the nitty gritty of a shoot, to have someone thinking in terms of imagery is great.”

Covid protocols meant San Martín could not be on the set of the film, a musical about the life of the late composer Jonathan Larson, but she could watch the shoot in real time from a monitor in the producers’ room in a nearby building, as well as follow the daily rushes. For her, the sheer scale of being on a major movie was inspiring. “I’d studied filmmaking at university in Buenos Aires without even having a camera,” she says. “So watching everything that was happening felt insane. I felt I want to play with these toys, I want to do this. It was eye-opening.”

name.value: Agustina San Martín, 2020-2022 OPEN CATEGORY PROTÉGÉÉ

title.value: I am focusing now on making a narrative for a broader audience. Lin-Manuel is a master of that. It has been wonderful having this conversation with him as I move into this new path for myself.

text.value:
The experience coincided with a change in her thinking. Confined to her flat in the early stages of the pandemic, she began to feel she wanted to move away from making authorial, independent films and towards those with a more mainstream narrative. “I’ve learned so many things from Lin-Manuel and from this mentorship,” she says.

“I am focusing now on making a narrative for a broader audience. Lin-Manuel is a master of that. It has been wonderful having this conversation with him as I move into this new path for myself.”

As far as Miranda is concerned, his most helpful contribution to San Martín’s growth as an artist has been to support her own process of thinking. “Just to ask questions like: ‘What’s the image you have in your mind? What’s the thing you want to communicate?’ As an outside observer, I can tell you how close or how far you are from that aim.”

title.value: Agustina’s art is infused with her curiosity and the essence of what she is after. I am really excited to see what she makes.

text.value:
He has also advised her on the balance between earning money as a struggling artist, with pursuing her own ideas. “That was the hardest thing for me early in my career, saying no to the good stuff so you can say yes to the best stuff. There’s the constant pressure of paying your rent and keeping your lights on.” But he is confident that San Martín has a bright future. “I just want her to be able to work at the scale she wants to work at,” he says. “I wish her the courage to press forward without necessarily knowing all the answers, but trusting she will find them along the way.”

San Martín now believes she has the confidence to follow her vision. “This mentorship has made me feel more empowered and more encouraged,” she says. “It’s like a stamp of quality.”

pageCaption.value: Relating to the experience of Native Americans in his country, Spike Lee chose to mentor Kyle Bell, a young filmmaker from the Thlopthlocco Creek Tribal Town of Oklahoma, who needed to break out of his shell.
